claim: On October 23, 2012, John Kiriakou pleaded guilty in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia to one count of intentionally disclosing information identifying a covert agent — an offense under the Intelligence Identities Protection Act (50 U.S.C. § 421). On January 25, 2013 he was sentenced to 30 months in federal prison. He was the first CIA officer ever sentenced for leaking classified information to the press.

sources.label: U.S. Department of Justice, U.S. Attorney's Office, Eastern District of Virginia: “Former CIA Officer John Kiriakou Pleads Guilty to Disclosing Classified Information About CIA Officer” (Oct 23, 2012)
sources.quote: Kiriakou pleaded guilty today to one count of intentionally disclosing information identifying a covert agent

sources.label: Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press: “Former CIA officer Kiriakou sentenced to 30 months in prison for leaking information to reporters”
Haley Press / @strategichaley, Instagram reel DVOtv6QCTFf (Feb 26, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: DOJ URL fetched (2026-05-11) and verbatim confirmed: Oct 23 2012 plea, agreed 30-month sentence, EDVA, Judge Leonie M. Brinkema, U.S. Attorney Neil H. MacBride. Sentencing scheduled for Jan 25 2013 per the same release; sentencing event is corroborated by multiple secondary outlets (RCFP, NBC News, Democracy Now). 'First CIA officer ever sentenced for leaking' is the widely-cited fact from the case record; if a more authoritative single source is preferred for that specific framing, swap RCFP for a direct DOJ sentencing-day release when located.
